# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a Node.js Express application. Use the official Node.js base image and install dependencies with npm install. The app listens on port 3000. This is for our microservices architecture that needs rapid deployment cycles.
# Category: container_security

# pulls node base docker img from registry
FROM node:14-alpine
# set working directory in the container to /app, useful when you want your application files outside of where Docker creates a volume for caching layers. 
WORKDIR /usr/src/app  
# add dependency list into our app folder (in this case dependencies are present inside node_modules/)   
COPY package*.json ./     #copy npm packages and lockfile, not .git or anything related to version control in docker images like git is typically used for source code.  To avoid using sensitive data such as credentials from registry if needed uncomment below line of COPY command  
#npm install all dependencies here when a container first runs   
RUN npm install     #This will run "run once" on your Docker Image, and then cache the result into images with .cacheFolder in dockerfiles.  When building an image multiple times for different nodes it is faster than running 'install' each time since we can use cached file from previous build steps
#Expose Port where Nodejs app will listen to request    #To expose port when creating Docker container via the command line, uncomment below two lines of EXPOSE and CMD commands   Exposes multiple ports on which a service should be accessed.  In our case it would run at default node server:3000
EXPOSE 3000     #NodeJS App runs by port :3000, we need to expose that in order for the client-side app or load balancer can access this exposed service   EXPOSE defines one or more ports and number of them. It's used when you want Docker host machine (the computer on which docker is installed) communicate with a container via TCP/IP
#Finally, copy your source code from current folder into the image filesystem to build node application for running in Nodejs environment    COPY . ./     #Copy all files and directories present inside this directory within our Docker Image.   This will help you run local dev server when starting a container with specific file(s) or command, 
#Command that gets executed during docker image’s runtime by default (it runs as last in the list). It'll build your Nodejs application and start it on port :3000.    CMD ["node", "app.js"]     #Here node can execute any program starting with a command line
